coredata.dc:title: Food for future: Exploring cutting-edge technology and practices in vertical farm

coredata.dc:description: With the growing urbanized population, vertical farming becomes indispensable in eradicating hunger and ensuring food security for the future. Food security has become a major challenge for the future owing to the scarcity of land, rising global population, urbanization, and polluted soil and water resources. In this scenario, vertical farming as an innovative food production technology caters increased productivity with limited resources and improved carbon footprint. The review begins with a brief introduction to the concept of vertical farming, its opportunities and implications on the economy, environment, and society as a whole, following with an in-depth qualitative analysis of the cutting-edge innovations/technologies are presented in detail. Contrary to the numerous research focusing on mere analysis of economic viability and statistical analysis, the aim is to introduce real cutting-edge technologies that address the challenges of vertical farms such as lack of technical skills or energy requirements. The recent advances of vertical farms includes construction of outer vertical structure and growing shelves using light weight and transparent polymer composites, engineered and tunable LEDs lightings, 3-D printed and recyclable growing substrates, AI-integrated IoTs for environment control, harvesting materials for renewable energy storage etc. Such material innovations with inclusion of AI and robotics transform automated vertical farming into ‘state of the art’ agricultural technology. Production shelf fabricated using UV cured GBOV-glass fiber composite with 60% transparency can overcome the material weight limitation, corrosion, light obstructions etc. associated with the conventional steel shelves. Replacing non-renewable fossil fuels using renewable energy harvested by carbon dots, fluorescent materials, and perovskite solar cells is recommended to minimize the high capital investments associated with energy as well as to reduce the environmental impact. Smart materials such as phase change materials and thermoresponsive windows control the amount of energy expended whereas smart substrate promotes resource management. Application of the newest technology AI in vertical farming facilitates precise control of environment, early pest detection, automation of farming processes, data-driven decision making and precision agriculture. In short, vertical farms utilizing modern innovations of science and technology caters enhanced productivity, improved quality, reduced cost, resource management and sustainability. Hence, automated and sustainable vertical farms promoting global food security and circularity have the potential to transform into burgeoning technology of future.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from BMBF project food4future (Funding code IGZ: 031B0730A, PYCO: 031B0730F) within the Funding Line Agricultural Systems of the Future and Fraunhofer IAP for the laboratory facilities. We are also grateful to Indo-German Science and Technology Center (IGSTC) Postdoctoral Fellowship for funding Dr. Swathi Erekath as a guest scientist in Fraunhofer IAP. We acknowledge Dr. Christian Rabe and Volker Eberhardt, department of Chromogenic Polymers, research division Functional Polymer Systems, Fraunhofer IAP for complying with the digital photographs and related information on thermochromic windows. The authors also thank Dr. Mathias Koehler, Dirk Dietrich and Tiberiu Tatai of Fraunhofer IAP for their assistance during the experimental work.

illustrating the materials and technologies involved.

(a) Edible insect mix containing crickets, grasshoppers, and mealworms sold commercially (b) Sea weeds consumed in the form of salad and commercially known as ‘Goma Wakame’ (c) Salicornia or glasswort.
Different types of vertical farming practiced such as hydroponics, aeroponics, and
aquaponics.
Comparison of open field production, greenhouse production, and vertical farming in
terms of water usage, crop yield and distance travelled by the food (EIT Food).
Vacuum infusion molding of transparent glass-fiber reinforced polymer composites.
Software-controlled UV exposure machine used for curing transparent polymer-glass
fiber composites. The instrument contains provisions for three different UV wavelengths
combined with dynamic movement of the exposure stage (Developed at the University of Applied Sciences Wildau).
(a) Refractive index of UV cured resin 2263+BMA (3:1)+ 1 wt% 2100+ 0.5 wt% VC
(GBOV) and 2/2 woven glass fiber fabric; (b) UV–VIS spectra of neat resin GBOV
and laminate prepared by reinforcing the same resin with 2/2 woven fabric. All the
resin-based systems were cured under 390 nm UV light for 10 s over a working distance
of 30 mm and intensity 2,721 mW/cm².

Digital images of (a) neat resin 2263+BMA (3:1)+ 1 wt% 2100+ 0.5 wt% VC (GBOV); (b) composite (5-layers) formed by reinforcing GBOV with 2/2 woven fabric. Both of them were cured under UV light of 390 nm for 10 s at a working distance of 30 mm and intensity 2,721 mW/cm².

(a) Schematic diagram of soilless farming illustrating plant growth on a substrate (Maluin et al., 2021); (b) and (c) represents molecular structure of zeolite (International Zeolite Association) and hydrogel (Aswathy et al., 2020) (Copyright © 2020 Published by Elsevier Ltd) respectively; (d) Digital images indicating growth of grass on textile fabric substrate (Bielefeld University of Applied Sciences 2019).
3D printed algae-laden alginate scaffolds. Reproduced with permission from Lode et al. (2015) Copyright © 2015, John Wiley and Sons.

Scanning electron microscopy images showing growth of macroalgae Ulva fenestrate at 3.5% salinity at 16 °C for 89 and 632 days and crickets Acheta domesticus at 32 °C and 70% relative humidity for 3 days starting from untreated and autoclaved textile substrates. M1 and M4 were polyester-based substrates whereas M2 and M3 were based on regenerative cellulose and PLA (polylactic acid) respectively. (a) Spectrum showing Photosynthetically Active Radiation (PAR) (The Ideal LED Grow Light Spectrum for Plants); and (b) Comparison of lettuce grown under red+blue light (left) and white light (right) (PAR Light & Spectral Quality).

RCDs and principle of antioxidant sunlight conversion film; (b) Digital images
showing growth of mung bean sprouts of the control and experimental group; (c) and
(d) show the comparison of the fresh, dry weight and the chlorophyll content of the
control and experimental group respectively (CK: control group with pure PVA film;
RCDs: experimental group with anti-oxidant RCDs/PVA film). Reproduced with
permission from Li et al. (2021). Copyright © 2021, Elsevier.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the growing urbanized population, vertical farming becomes indispensable in eradicating hunger and ensuring food security for the future. Food security has become a major challenge for the future owing to the scarcity of land, rising global population, urbanization, and polluted soil and water resources. In this scenario, vertical farming as an innovative food production technology caters increased productivity with limited resources and improved carbon footprint. The review begins with a brief introduction to the concept of vertical farming, its opportunities and implications on the economy, environment, and society as a whole, following with an in-depth qualitative analysis of the cutting-edge innovations/technologies are presented in detail. Contrary to the numerous research focusing on mere analysis of economic viability and statistical analysis, the aim is to introduce real cutting-edge technologies that address the challenges of vertical farms such as lack of technical skills or energy requirements. The recent advances of vertical farms includes construction of outer vertical structure and growing shelves using light weight and transparent polymer composites, engineered and tunable LEDs lightings, 3-D printed and recyclable growing substrates, AI-integrated IoTs for environment control, harvesting materials for renewable energy storage etc. Such material innovations with inclusion of AI and robotics transform automated vertical farming into ‘state of the art’ agricultural technology. Production shelf fabricated using UV cured GBOV-glass fiber composite with 60% transparency can overcome the material weight limitation, corrosion, light obstructions etc. associated with the conventional steel shelves. Replacing non-renewable fossil fuels using renewable energy harvested by carbon dots, fluorescent materials, and perovskite solar cells is recommended to minimize the high capital investments associated with energy as well as to reduce the environmental impact. Smart materials such as phase change materials and thermoresponsive windows control the amount of energy expended whereas smart substrate promotes resource management. Application of the newest technology AI in vertical farming facilitates precise control of environment, early pest detection, automation of farming processes, data-driven decision making and precision agriculture. In short, vertical farms utilizing modern innovations of science and technology caters enhanced productivity, improved quality, reduced cost, resource management and sustainability. Hence, automated and sustainable vertical farms promoting global food security and circularity have the potential to transform into burgeoning technology of future.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Innovative food production approaches forms the new era of research and technology owing to the increasing global demand for food supply with the limited land available for cultivation (Benke & Tomkins, 2017; Ramin Shamshiri et al., 2018). Population growth along with urbanization have led to reduction in arable land available for cultivation fueling food insecurity globally. Moreover, climatic changes and environmental pollution affects the naturally available resources such as fresh water and soil producing resource scarcity (Al-Chalabi, 2015). Substantial decrease in the percentage of population practicing traditional agriculture is yet another threat for the reduced food supplies. All these factors collectively have envisioned the need for modernized farming technologies (Goldstein et al., 2016). Recently, conventional agriculture practices are viewed with skepticism due to its impact on environment including climatic changes, biodiversity loss and degradation of land and freshwater (Foley et al., 2011). The need of the hour is a transition towards newer farming technologies amalgamating food security and environmental conservation.

Contemplating the present scenario of global food security, there is a devastating rise in the problem of food shortage with the advent of COVID-19 pandemic (Hume et al., 2022). The figures presented by the 2022 report of United Nation's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) (Global Food Worries - UN's FAO Economist Monika Tothova, 2022) are alarming. The report outlines a total of 828 million people affected by hunger in 2021, which was around 150 million more than that in 2019. This rise can be understood to be due to the discontinued food production, and constraints in transport and storage during the lockdown periods (The World Bank, 2022). Further, Monika Tothova, an economist at FAO expressed the concern of UN over the conflict in Ukraine, which normally export grains to 400 million people around the world (Global Food Worries - UN's FAO Economist Monika Tothova, 2022). The war has interfered with the food supplies across the borders thereby aggravating the problem of food security (Global Food Worries - UN's FAO Economist Monika Tothova, 2022). Inflation is yet another factor contributing to the problem of food security. Latest updates from World Bank suggests an inflation in the domestic food prices at an alarming rate in almost all countries. For instance, the inflation is greater than 5 percent in 70.6% of low-income, 81.4% of lower-middle-income and 84% of upper-middle-income countries. Surprisingly, inflation affected food prices the most and constituted 84.5% of the overall inflation (The World Bank, 2023). In short, the effects of COVID-19, Ukraine war, and inflation have aggravated the problem of food security. In addition, with less transparency in food growing conditions such as amounts of fertilizers, pesticides, and time taken by crops to reach the stores, consumption of nutritious and healthy food has become an anticipation. To comprehend, health and food safety have become an issue of major concern that can be overcome by switching to urban farming.

Urban farming includes vertical farming, greenhouse farming, container farming, rooftop farming, spared field farming, and warehouse farming (Xi et al., 2022). Among these, vertical farming is attaining increased attention owing to the production of high value crops with higher yield and its eco-friendly impact on the environment (Rajan et al., 2019). Vertical farming is the practice of growing crops vertically by artificially stacking them in unused urban spaces or rooftops. The artificial growth further requires provision for optimizing and controlling plant growth conditions (Despommier, 2010). Tracing the history of vertical farming, the idea of producing food in multi-storey buildings was first presented by Othmar Ruthner way back in 1966 (Othmar, 1966). However, the concept of vertical farming to grow crops either horizontally or vertically in multi-storeys with controlled environment was first introduced by Dickson Despommier in 2009 (Despommier, 2010). Vertical cultivation of crops in tall buildings enables maximum utilization of space with limited resources thereby promising food security for the future along with sustainability. Access to fresh, healthy and transparently produced food, fewer food miles, reduced food wastage during transportation and improved carbon footprint is an added advantage (Despommier, 2011). A schematic of a vertical farm illustrating the materials and technologies involved is given in Fig. 1 . The inclination of consumers interest towards ‘growing own food’ with limited available space gives scope for widespread practice of vertical farming. The costumer's preference over local food can be appreciated from a survey conducted by the National Restaurant Association, United States. Their survey reports an increase in local food sales from $4.8 billion in 2011 to nearly $12 billion in 2014 (Tropp, 2014). This local food predilection of the consumers and restaurants further intensified with the advent of COVID-19 pandemic (Cappelli et al., 2022). The opportunities of vertical farming will be further discussed in detail in Section 3.

The contribution of vertical farms to food security and sustainable agriculture is highly recommendable that the global vertical farming market size in 2023 was around USD 5.1 billion, which is expected to become 15.3 billion by 2028 (MarketsandMarkets). The markets are dominated by East and Southeast Asia and North America, including China, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, the USA, and Canada (Ramin Shamshiri et al., 2018) with more than 33% of the share. However, Europe and Middle Eastern countries have recently emerged as the major competitors (Kabir et al., 2023). Some specific examples of successful vertical farming projects are explained in Section 5. There are approximately 200 vertical farms in Japan growing vegetables like tomatoes, bell peppers, lettuce, and broccoli. The most prominent are Tokyo-based companies like Spread Co., Ltd., Kyoto, Japan and Mirai Co., Ltd., Gifu Prefecture (Countries Using Vertical Farming). China has around 250 active vertical farming sites (Wen) with SANANBIO being the lead producer of over 300 varieties of leafy greens, fruits, medicinal herbs, and edible flowers (Chinese startup gets in on ground floor of high-rise farms). South Korea is known for its smart vertical farms such as N.Thing, NextOn, Farm 8, and ALGA Farmtech (John) and a prototype Suwon utilizing renewable energy sources for heating, cooling, and lighting is under development (M. Levenston). USA dominates the market with their major contributors being 80 Acres Farms (80 ACRES FARMS), AeroFarms (AEROFARM), AmHydro, BrightFarms (BRIGHT FARMS), Hort Americas, Plenty, and The Plant (The Plant). Recently, Europe has become a major contributor with the increasing demand for fresh and healthy food with environmental sustainability. Netherlands being the leader with its vertical farms such as PlantLab (Plantlab), UrbanFarmers (Urban Farmers), and B-Four Agro are followed by Agricool of Paris and Infarm from Berlin (Infarm). Food4future (f4f), one of the vertical farming projects under implementation in Germany not only focuses on vertical farming of crops but also on cultivating algaes and insects (Fricke et al., 2022).

Alongside vertical farming, a new practice to ensure food security is by cultivating nutritious organisms such as macroalgae, halophytes and edible insects. Identification of sustainable and natural food sources is required to address the shortages in crop cultivation and animal rearing. Insufficient arable land and global climatic changes further pressurize the need for new, nature-based food sources complementing nutritional requisites with environmental protection. Acceptance of edible insects and algae as a part of human diet is a major breakthrough in this regard. Eating insects and seaweed is not new to humans and were part of the diet of people in Asia, Latin America, and Africa from ancient past (Magara et al., 2021). Edible algae such as seaweed, Spirulina, and Chlorella were consumed directly or were incorporated as ingredients in a wide variety of food products (Mabeau & Fleurence, 1993; Norziah & Ching, 2000; Tso et al., 2020). In recent times, the Western people too have experienced a transition in their dietary habits with the European Union approving the sale of insects for human consumption in January 2023 (Logan, 2023). Such natural organisms are important considering their high nutritious value and limited resources required for their growth. For instance, seaweeds are rich in proteins, minerals, amino acids and lipids necessary for human metabolism (Mišurcová, 2011; Sartal et al., 2011). Their high content of bioactive compounds makes them beneficial as pharmaceutical and medicinal products (MacArtain et al., 2008). As mentioned, farming edible insects require less water and feed as compared to chicken and the amount of carbon dioxide produced is lesser, owing to the nutritional benefits and environment sustainability, insects and algae have been accepted as an alternative nutritious diet globally promoting food security and scope for new business opportunities. Through the project ‘food4future’ (f4f), a BMBF (Federal Ministry of Education and Research, Germany) project the amalgamation of vertical farming technology to farm natural food sources like algae and crickets is attempted to promote sustainable biomass production. Despite the acceptance, farming of these organisms is considered a major challenge, which we try to address by adopting vertical farming practice which provides controlled growth conditions. Fig. 2 shows different varieties of commercially sold edible insects and algae purchased by us as a part of f4f project.

In this review, we attempt to apprehend the contribution of vertical farming in providing a sustainable and healthy solution to the problem of continued food supply. In spite of material and technological innovations being the key for successful implementation of vertical farms, there is a serious dearth of literatures discussing the relevance of it in the context of vertical farming. An elaborated research conducted by Dsouza et al. very recently on the existing literatures points out the dominance of photo-biological research and lack of socio-economic and technical reviews (Dsouza et al., 2023). Here, we try to abridge this gap by providing an in-depth understanding of the state-of-the-art innovations in different aspects of vertical farms such as outer structure, growing shelves, growth substrates, artificial lighting, and environment control and monitoring (see Fig. 1). Some of the practical applications of these materials in existing vertical farms are illustrated along with their impact on economics and sustainability. Throughout the review, our focus was to identify low cost, biobased and biodegradable, energy saving materials to replace the conventional materials in traditional farming. The implications of state-of-the-art technologies like artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning and robotics in creating automated vertical farms are highlighted. Application of renewable energy sources, waste to energy conversion and water harvesting are emphasized as cleaner ways to reduce environmental impact and economic feasibility of vertical farms. Contrary to the existing reviews on vertical farming, the focus is to promote increased acceptance to the idea of vertical farming aiming food security and sustainability. The review also includes findings from our f4f project and include proposals to adapt some of the already existing technologies to vertical farming.

It is important to understand that vertical farming is soilless cultivation. Vertical farming innovations are of different types such as hydroponics, aquaponics and aeroponics or one or more of these combined. Fig. 3 gives a schematic illustration of hydroponics, aeroponics and aquaponics. As the name suggests, hydroponics uses nutrient-rich water solution as the growing medium for the plants replacing soil in conventional farming (Kalantari et al., 2017; Shahda & Megahed, 2023). The different forms of indoor hydroponic systems includes Nutrient Film Technique (NFT), Wick System, Water Culture, Ebb and Flow (Flood and Drain), Drip Feed System and Aeroponic Systems (Ma et al., 2023). Here, all the vital nutrients are provided through water, making nutrient absorption easier. This results in larger produce with high nutritional value within shorter times (Ellis; Voss, 2012). Generally, gray water (water used for showers or washing hands) or water from the roof of buildings is filtered, used and re-circulated in the system thereby conserving the freshwater resources. Hydroponics uses 70% lesser water than that used by open agriculture. Further, the agricultural run-off and leaching is impeded by re-circulation of nutrient solution. Another main advantage of this system is reduced soil-borne diseases assuring quality of crops due to controlled administering of nutrients. However, the main disadvantage is their higher installation costs and lack of technical skills required to attain maximum yield and product quality (Rajan et al., 2019; Savvas, 2003). In aeroponics, plants suspended in deep air are sprayed with a fine mist of nutrient solution unlike hydroponics where nutrient solution is used as the growth medium. Aeroponics offer all the advantages as that of hydroponics with upper hand in water conservation. Aeroponics use 70 – 90% lesser water as compared to hydroponics (Despommier, 2010). Further, the roots can access more oxygen and can grow freely as they are exposed to the environment (Rajan et al., 2019). This also prevents pathogen formation on the roots and can save 60% of nutrients and 100% of fertilizers and pesticides (Mirzabe et al., 2022). The growth of crops in aeroponics is usually determined by the aerosol droplet size. The disadvantages is that any failure in the system would stop the flow of nutrients resulting in crop failure (Rajan et al., 2019; Savvas, 2003).

Aquaponics amalgamates hydroponics and aquaculture by cultivating fish and crops simultaneously in a recirculating system. It thereby eliminates the negative effects of hydroponics and aquaculture. For instance, the need to dispose organic waste excreted by the fish is managed by supplying to the crops. As the waste is rich in nutrients such as ammonia, it can be utilized as a biofertilizer. The plant roots then filter the wastewater, which is pumped back to the fish tank for reuse (Krastanova et al., 2022). Thus, aquaponics eliminates the need for solid waste disposal which otherwise is costly and interferes with the environment. Aquaponic systems are safe, easy to operate and requires less monitoring than conventional techniques. The major advantage of aquaponics is that plant and fish species can be chosen based on the market demand (Krastanova et al., 2022; Masser et al.,). The disadvantage of aquaponics is the high investment cost and complications within the system. This includes the addition of bases to wastewater to neutralize the acids present in fish fecal matter and maintaining the oxygen saturation of the water. Further, any failure in power or blockage can destroy both fish and crops (Rajan et al., 2019; Partridge 2013).

Albeit the opportunities offered by these different systems, the major challenges are high cost of installation, lack of technical skills, high energy input and abrupt malfunctioning of the system. Our review covers some materialistic possibilities to overcome some of these challenges using newer technologies.

In order to appreciate the significance of vertical farming in providing food for future, the opportunities need to be elucidated in detail. Hence, the following section is imperative in making vertical farming equally acceptable and adaptable by common man.

An important critical edge of vertical farm is in utilization of maximum space augmented by maximum yield per square meter. This can be assimilated from the statistics presented by various literatures comparing vertical farms with green houses and open agriculture. Gerrewey et al. state that the lettuce yield per square meter were observed to be higher in vertical farms, which was 80 times than that in open-field agriculture and 12 times of a greenhouse (Graamans et al., 2018; Touliatos et al., 2016; Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). The higher yield per square meter observed can be attributed to the stacked crop growth with minimalistic influence of weather and climatic conditions. Yield as high as an annual wheat produce of 1940 ± 230 t/ha is predicted by harvesting from a 10-layer indoor vertical facility located in a single hectare of land. This predicted yield is equivalent to 600 times the current world average annual wheat produce (Asseng et al., 2020). As crop grown in open farms are exposed to harsh weather conditions such as floods and droughts and microbial infections such as pest attacks, there arises higher chances for crop damage and wastage. While vertical farms eliminate such losses due to the controlled growth conditions resulting in higher production yield. A vertical farm with multi-level design is believed to provide more growing area, to be precise, eight times as in a single level greenhouse or open farm for the same floor area (Sarkar & Majumder, 2015). Further, the controlled environment with optimum growth parameters facilitates versatile crop production throughout the year irrespective of the season. For example, Aerofarm, a large indoor vertical farm in New Jersey for vegetables produces 22 different varieties in a year. With the advent of advanced technology such as automation and data analysis, higher yield with shorter growing cycle is achieved with several sources claiming e. g. a total of two weeks for lettuce production (Pinstrup-Andersen, 2018). The technological advancements in the area will be discussed in detail thereafter. Conclusively, larger, faster, and uninterrupted food supply to cater the demands of the rising population is assured by vertical farming technology.

Catering healthy and nutritious food is yet another attractive facet of vertical farming. An important shortcoming of traditional farming is the use of pesticides to prevent crop failure compromising the health of the consumers. Growing crop in vertical farms with controlled environment and without soil, fertilizers, and pesticides promises healthy and nutritious food. This is particularly important as more than 25% of the world population is estimated to suffer from micronutrient deficiencies and associated health problems (Pinstrup-Andersen, 2018). In addition, there is a serious concern over nutritional loss in conventional farming as the food travel long distances before reaching the consumers. Given the fact that leafy green crops loses its nutritional value with time, the time taken by the food to reach costumers is a matter of serious concern (Martin & Bustamante, 2021; Pandrangi & LaBorde, 2006). This time delay is avoided in vertical farms as it operates close to the consumers thereby ensuring food freshness and nutritional quality. Vertically produced crops are considered fresh to such an extent that in countries like the USA, these are marketed as organic products (Organic Trade Association OTA. Hydroponics. 2019). The kind of transparency offered by this vertical farming system is making it increasingly acceptable to the public. Curtailing the transport of food over long distances provides other advantages as well. It reduces the food price by cutting the costs associated with transportation, storage, and refrigeration. Further, food wastage during transportation is controlled to a greater extent.

Another important consideration is the effect of vertical farming on the environment. Reduced miles of food transport saves fuel thereby reducing the carbon footprint (Benke & Tomkins, 2017). Environmentalists consider supplying food to the cities as one of the major contributors of greenhouse gas emissions, biodiversity loss, water pollution etc. Moreover, urban farming contributes to environmental conservation by minimizing the need for freshwater resources and soil as already discussed in Section 2. Large percent of ground water resources are continuously exploited for crop irrigation in traditional farming. Gerrewey et al. suggest that the water usage of a vertical farm is observed to be 18 times lower than of an open-field agriculture (Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). Also, it is lower than in greenhouses as they require water in their fogging system used for cooling purposes. Another associated ecological advantage is the minimum release of chemicals to the environment. Enormous amounts of fertilizers and pesticides released to the environment in an open farm not only pollutes the soil but also leaching of these chemicals to freshwater bodies can be detrimental to aquatic life. As vertical farming involves re-circulation of nutrient solution, it prevents the release of any harmful chemicals to the surroundings. Therefore, the contribution of vertical farming towards nature and ecology is highly commendable.

More precisely, the main challenge facing traditional agriculture such as the unavailability of arable land due to urbanization is addressed by farming vertically in multi-stacks on rooftops or abandoned, unused spaces. Another shortcoming of traditional farming is the dependence on seasonal changes and unpredictable weather conditions. This is eliminated in a vertical farm ensuring higher yields crop production all-round the year in artificially controlled climatic environment. Moreover, the impact of conventional agriculture on environment is devastating. It depends heavily on large-scale, industrialized farming methods involving extensive use of land, water, and chemicals. Such extensive use not only create resource scarcity but also leads to increased deforestation, soil degradation, water pollution, and greenhouse gas emissions (Srinivasan & Yadav, 2023). Vertical farming follows a more environmentally friendly approach for growing food with minimal resources and no harmful chemicals. The relevance of vertical farming promising food security for future is summarized in Fig. 4 by comparing it with conventional agriculture and greenhouse farming. As a whole, vertical farming as a newer technology promises increased supply of healthy and nutritious food with fewer resources amalgamated with minimum impact on land, water and biodiversity.

Vertical farms have certain economic advantages over conventional open farms. Because of the stacked growth, the yield is very high from unit area of land. Crops are also produced round-the-year in controlled environment without the effect of weather conditions. These are discussed in detail in Section 3. Furthermore, vertical farming cuts the transportation and storage costs as the farms are located close to the consumers. Vertical farms even host restaurants or groceries where the products can be purchased directly.

However, the economic feasibility of a vertical farm should be overlooked as higher initial investment as well as operating costs are involved. This involves the cost of land in urban areas, energy required for lighting and environment control, labor, cost of supplies, harvest and post-harvest storage (Lubna et al., 2022). The construction costs are very high such that the initial investment cost per square meter of growing space is ten times higher than a high-tech greenhouse (Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). The expenses involved in electricity particularly that required for artificial lighting and environment control follows thereby making energy usage the largest contributor of the operating costs. Even if there exist public policies providing necessary capital investments for establishing vertical farms, it lacks in terms of operating costs to run the farms profitably (Van Delden et al., 2021). Hence, fast growing crops with short height and large ratio of salable plant parts such as leafy greens, microgreens, and herbs are currently grown in vertical farms, making them cost-effective (Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). On examining the feasibility and plausibility of vertical farming concepts, Malek Al-Chalabi suggests installations of solar panels on the roof and facade of the farm to meet the energy requirements for lighting and irrigation (Al-Chalabi, 2015). Further, a life cycle analysis showed a carbon footprint of five times for vertically grown lettuce in summer than the conventional open fields (Al-Chalabi, 2015). Utilizing unused spaces or rooftops for creating such farms can reduce the land costs involved. Integration of technologies and materials discussed in detail throughout Section 6 and 7 such as polymer composites, use of renewable energy sources, light and water harvesting etc. is believed to provide cost-effectiveness.

A brief overview of the major environmental contributions by vertical farms are already discussed in Section 3 along with its opportunities. Reduced use of land and water protects the ecosystem while decreased food transportation over miles reduce carbon footprint. The use of pesticides and fertilizers are negligible thereby preventing its release to the environment (Kabir et al., 2023; Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). Mostly, vertical farms utilize domestic wastewater for irrigation preserving freshwater resources. Further, closed loop vertical farming system produce circularity by allowing nutrient solution to recirculate preventing their release to the environment as well as minimum water usage. Hence, eutrophication of aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems produced by leaching of nitrogen and phosphorous in open agriculture is controlled to a larger extent in vertical farms (Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). A reduction of 70 to 90% regarding eutrophication is expected in vertical farms as compared to open-field agriculture (Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). However, the energy intensive operations such as artificial lighting and environmental control poses a negative impact on the environment (Kabir et al., 2023). The innovations proposed in Section 6 and 7 such as harnessing renewable energy resources, water-harvesting, automated control, and recyclable substrates from waste plastics etc. can contribute to a sustainable ecosystem.

The social and cultural implications of vertical farming can be visualized through the food security achieved and employment opportunities created in the society. Vertical farming ensures food security by making food available locally in spite of the reduction in arable land and resource scarcity. Such locally grown foods are fresh and do not involve nutritional losses making healthy and nutritious diet available to the public. Further, they are grown in controlled environments without any pesticides or insecticides. This makes the vertically produced food more relatable to organic food produced as already discussed in Section 1 (Organic Trade Association OTA. Hydroponics. 2019). Another important social implication is the creation of many new and independent entrepreneurs. Recent years have seen a growth in venture capitalists and start-ups practicing urban farming (Van Delden et al., 2021). There are many policies supporting these entrepreneurs such as tax adjustments or subsidies provided to promote their growth. The advanced technologies needed, such as LEDs, AI and IoTs provides further scope for start-ups. Further, setting up a vertical farm employs a multi-disciplinary team of architects, engineers, scientists, farmers, horticulturists, environmentalists, marketers, and economists. Engineers are required for tower construction, solar panel installation while software developers are involved in data analysis and predictions (Al-Kodmany, 2018).

Most of the vertical farms hosts groceries and restaurants that makes growers and consumers closely connected with each other. This allows the growers to understand the requirements of the consumers. Vertical farms are also known to conduct educational and training programs. For instance, the vertical farm of Singapore ‘Sky Greens’ conducts educational programs where students are allowed to visit the farm and gain hands-on experience in transplanting and harvesting, enabling them to appreciate the significance of science and technology in creating green urban solutions (Al-Kodmany, 2018). One of the shortcomings is that vertical farming creates unequal opportunities, more focused towards urban populations with displacement effects on rural areas (Van Delden et al., 2021).

Sky Greens in Singapore is the world's pioneering low-carbon, hydraulic-driven vertical farm (Kabir et al., 2023; Sky Greens). It uses the innovative A-Go-Gro technology that consists of rotating A-shaped aluminum frames with 38 tiers of growing trough in six square meters of space (Chatterjee et al., 2020). The rotating system ensures that the plants receive uniform sunlight, irrigation, and nutrients. It is powered by a recycled water-pulley system that deploys rainwater harvested from their overhead reservoirs. Furthermore, these farms harness natural sunlight and also derives energy from organic waste composted.

AeroFarms is a pioneering innovative indoor vertical farm located in New Jersey, USA revolutionizing global agriculture through adoption of aeroponics (Kabir et al., 2023; AEROFARM). Their mission is centered around delivery of community-nurtured delicious food grown using an environmentally friendly approach. AeroFarms is at the forefront of technological advancements by automating R&D, optimizing plant biology, customizing lighting arrays, and capturing smart data to reduce future farm costs and investments (Kabir et al., 2023). The technological advancements are discussed later in Section 6 and 7.

Farm.One, an urban farm located in New York, USA grows unique luxury salad greens, specialty herbs, edible flowers and micro greens (Farm.One). They serve customers as well as chefs from the finest restaurants in New York City with their rarest culinary herbs (Farm.One; Agritecture). Additionally, they are engaged in various social activities such as organizing plant visits and educational lectures, training programs for the public, allowing them to appreciate the significance of urban farming. They grow food with zero pesticides and pack them in reusable containers, eliminating single use plastic packaging waste aiming net carbon zero farming in the long term (Farm.One).

Vertical Harvest is a three-story vertical farm in Jackson, Wyoming, USA practising hydroponics to produce fresh, local food (Vertical Harvest; Mainwaring). They cultivate microgreens, lettuce and tomato supplying around 100,000 pounds of fresh products to the market annually (Mainwaring). They provide amalgamation of technology, environment and social impact by leveraging a mix of public-private-partnerships with a customized, inclusive employment model focused on individuals with physical and/or intellectual disabilities (Vertical Harvest).

Contrary to the enormous benefits and sustainability offered by vertical farming, the economic concern associated with the installation of vertical farms and lack of technical skills can hinder the popularity of vertical farms as an alternative to conventional farms. Innovations in the field of materials and technology can control this problem largely. For instance, innovations in sensors and communication devices, use of renewable natural energy sources to reduce power consumption and costs. Smart materials that can adapt to the controlled environment to provide best optimal growth conditions, advances in vertical tower designs, and technologies to monitor and predict the optimum growth conditions, are imperative thereby revolutionizing the field of vertical farming. Considering the role of technological advancements and material innovations in rendering vertical farms sustainable and user-friendly, the purpose of the following section is to track the evolution of such developments.

The outer wall of any building hosting a vertical plant growth system is critical, as vertical farms require controlled environment. Different plant species require specific temperature for their growth and development and any variation from this temperature hinders their growth and results in loss of nutritional value or crop failure. To regulate the inside temperature, thermally insulated installations and airtight structures with cavity walls, foam concrete, loose fill insulation, light weight aggregate etc. are employed that form the outer structure. It helps in maintaining the interior temperature, humidity and carbon dioxide thereby reducing the energy requirements and associated costs (Avgoustaki & Xydis, 2020; Vatistas et al., 2022; Balachandar & Sidharthan, 2014). The thermally insulated walls are usually opaque with a high R-value to prevent thermal energy losses. The R-value is the ability of a material to resist heat flow and is an indication of its efficiency in thermal insulation. Wilson et al. proposed the use of ‘condenser tower’ typology for vertical farms in Dubai to harvest water from humidity in the arid regions. This design utilizes atmospheric humidity to grow plants along with climatic control in water-scarce regions (Wilson, 2009).

Vertical farms can also be constructed outdoors such as on rooftops, facades, corridors or window panes of existing high-rise buildings, restaurants and grocery stores (Mahdavi et al., 2012). Crops in such farms will be exposed to semi-controlled atmosphere as in greenhouses and involve challenges in achieving solar emissivity and controlled environment. Though, the cladding materials of green houses such as flat polycarbonate, single glass, and polytetrafluoroethylene can be employed (Vatistas et al., 2022), there arises the problem of temperature, humidity and light regulation increasing the heating requirements. Further, farming on rooftops is a serious concern due to the material weight limitations associated with the building's load capacity and light obstructions caused by shadows from buildings and other infrastructure (Muñoz-Liesa et al., 2022). To overcome these limitations, we propose to replace the conventional materials used for outer cover by light-weight and transparent composites based on glass fiber reinforced polymer (GFRP) using vacuum infusion molding as a part of the project f4f. Despommier et al. have envisioned the construction of a totally transparent building exclusively for growing crops (Despommier, 2011).

Affordability and accessibility being the major challenges of vertical farming (Van Gerrewey et al., 2021), the project f4f aims to develop a continuous food supply for the future addressing such challenges. The idea is to create flexible modular compartments for farming algae, halophytes, and crickets in unused urban spaces. GFRP composites provide superior material properties in terms of transparency, energy absorption, impact resistance, fatigue endurance, corrosion resistance, and long-term durability (Abdurohman et al., 2018). Hence, outer covers made from GFRP composites can be long lasting with provision for natural sunlight along with temperature control. The high specific strength of polymer composites, in general is promising regarding the weight limitations of the existing building. The transparent GFRP composites can be produced by vacuum infusion molding of epoxy resins impregnating glass fiber mats at room temperature. Vacuum infusion process provides with the advantage of lesser costs, fast processing and are also suited for larger area components (Abdurohman et al., 2018; Goren & Atas, 2008). Fig. 5 shows the vacuum infusion molding technique used for the fabrication of transparent epoxy-glass fiber composites.

One major characteristic that differentiate vertical farms from greenhouses is their tall, integrated structure with multiple stacked layers acting as shelves or racks for growing plants (Avgoustaki & Xydis, 2020). A vertical system usually consists of 4 to 15 rows with each shelf separated by a distance depending on the size of the crop grown. Usually metal shelves are employed, with steel being the conventional material. Steel is used because of the mechanical strength required to support increased weight of the artificial light system needed for plant growth (Vatistas et al., 2022). However, steel structures are heavier and can affect the static stability and thus can exceed the load capacity requisites of the building. This makes vertical farms practically unsuitable, particularly in already occupied spaces like rooftops or commercial buildings. As a part of the project f4f, we propose to replace the heavier steel shelves by a novel transparent glass fiber reinforced polymer composite produced by UV-curing. The following key findings are reported in this section.

As mentioned, the objective is to address the problem of high-cost and heavy weight associated with the vertical structural design. Polymeric composites provide a synergistic combination of low weight and high strength among the existing conventional materials along with better dimensional stability, chemical and heat resistance (Goren & Atas, 2008; Kessler, 2012). The superiority of GFRPs in providing improved impact and fatigue resistance with long-term durability (Abdurohman et al., 2018) is already discussed in Section 6.1. It is already known that composites with properties similar to that of steel and stiffness higher than aluminum, with just one-quarter of specific gravity of steel can be obtained by proper selection of glass fibers and orientation (Ramzan & Ehsan; Sathishkumar et al., 2014). Apart from the mechanical strength, GFRP composites provide provision for integrating light emitting diodes (LEDs) and sensor modules facilitating artificial light and environment control respectively. The inherent transparency of the GFRP shelves can ensure adequate light whether it be the artificial light from LEDs or the natural sunlight. Further, it minimizes the light obstruction caused by the conventional steel structures. Conclusively, GFRP composite shelves are expected to reduce the number of LEDs employed, thereby cutting the power requirements and associated cost. Corrosion being a major shortcoming of metal shelves demands frequent cleaning or replacement due to structural failure, particularly when saline environments are employed for halophyte or algae cultivation. The newly developed composite materials can address these challenges associated with the conventionally used steel shelves making vertical farms economically viable and socially acceptable.

In this work, we employed UV-curing to fabricate GFRP composites; where a monomer/oligomer resin formulation mixed with a photoinitiator is exposed to specific UV wavelengths to produce a highly crosslinked polymer network, thereby improving its final properties (Endruweit et al., 2006). The photopolymerization results in faster reaction kinetics producing polymeric resins with tailored physical, chemical, and mechanical properties. UV-curing of composites is highly advantageous due to faster cure rate, increased productivity, reduced floor space, elimination of solvents, reduced styrene emissions, minimum power requirements, and minimum wastage due to resin dripping (Fertier et al., 2013). Therefore, UV-curing is considered as an environmentally friendly and energy saving alternative to thermal curing of FRPs. Fig. 6 shows the software-controlled UV exposure machine employed for fabricating GFRPs in this particular study. It provides exposure from three different wavelengths simultaneously with the provision for automatically controlling the lamp positions as well as sample stage positions. A hand lay-up technique was used to fabricate the composites with acrylic resins forming the matrix and glass fiber fabric being the reinforcement. Acrylic resins can be UV-cured easily due to the high reactivity of their acrylate double bonds. Furthermore, low cost of acrylates along with their stability in seawater and resistance to hydrolysis and weathering makes them attractive candidates as matrix for our composites.

A significant challenge encountered in fabricating transparent GFRPs is the loss of transmitted light due to scattering. To minimize such losses, it is important to match the refractive index of the fiber with that of the polymeric matrix (Caydamli et al., 2021). A 2 × 2 twill woven glass fiber fabric with an area weight of 163 g/m2 and 9 – 68 tex of both warp and wept yarns were used as the reinforcement. Epoxy acrylates were chosen as the matrix polymer due to their inherent relatively high refractive indices. In addition, the chemical resistance and hydrolytic stability of epoxy acrylates are much better than of most conventional polyester acrylates. Fig. 7 (a) shows the refractive index matching of the 2/2 woven fabric with the proposed resin formulation cured under UV LED-light of 390 nm with an intensity of 2,721 mW/cm2 for 10 s. The refractive index of the glass fabric was determined using solvent mixtures of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and 1-bromonaphthalene in varying concentrations and were found to be around 1.567 at a wavelength of 548 nm. The proposed resin formulation contains Genomer 2263 (Bisphenol A epoxy diacrylate) as oligomer, benzyl methacrylate (BMA) as reactive diluent, 9-vinyl carbazole (VC) as optical dopant and Omnirad 2100 (mixture of TPO-L (Ethyl (2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl) phenylphosphinate) and Irgacure 819) as photoinitiator. It is important to note that the resin formulations were cured under UV light of wavelength 390 nm for 10 s at an intensity of 2,721 mW/cm2 and a working distance of 30 mm for refractive index measurement. It was observed that the resin system formed by homogeneously mixing 3 parts of Genomer 2263 with 1 part of BMA and containing 1 wt% of Omnirad 2100 and 0.5 wt% VC (GBOV) showed a refractive index overlap with woven fabric at a wavelength of 548 nm. The refractive index was found to be around 1.567 at 548 nm for both fabric and resin which is in the visible range. The refractive index matching is an important factor controlling the transparency of the final composite. Hence, the composite produced by impregnating the woven fabric with the proposed resin formulation GBOV will show good transparency.

Fig. 7(b) depict the transparencies of both GFRP laminates produced by impregnating five layers of fabric with resin GBOV and the pure resin GBOV, cured under 390 nm UV light of intensity 2,721 mW/cm2 for 10 s. The neat resin GBOV shows a transparency of about 80% over a wavelength range of 350 – 950 nm. The transparency obtained is comparable that of polymer diffusing films employed in optical components such as organic LEDs as reported by Eiselt et al. (2016). A transparency of 60% was observed for the woven fabric reinforced resin initiating from a wavelength of 400 nm. The reduced transparency of the composite as compared to the neat resin is due to the scattering arising from the multiple layers of glass fabric used as reinforcement, which is not perfectly index-matched for all visible wavelengths and some inhomogeneities. The transparency of the neat resin and composite produced can be appreciated further from the digital images given in Fig. 8 (a) and 8(b) respectively. The transparency of the neat resin GBOV is obvious from Fig. 8(a). However, Fig. 8(b) clearly shows that the final composite appears to be highly transparent at the center with almost no evidence of visible fibers whereas some fiber structures can be visualized toward its end making the composite translucent in those regions. This is because the center is completely wetted with the resin whereas the end portions are partially impregnated with the resin. Towards the corners, pristine fiber mat can be observed where the resin did not wet the fiber completely. Conclusively, the composite becomes transparent if GBOV resin impregnates the 2/2 woven fabric despite having 5 layers of the fiber mats. This GBOV-based glass fiber composite fabricated by UV-curing can be a prospective candidate for the multi-cultivation racks employed in vertical farms replacing the conventional steel. Transparent growing shelves eliminate the problem of upper layers shading the lower ones thereby eliminating or at least reducing the need for shelf rotation. Also, placement of light emitting diodes (LEDs) can be flexible and even provides the possibility to be integrated into the transparent composites.

A growth substrate is a media/material for growing plants artificially with characteristic similar to that of soil. They are integral for plant growth particularly in vertical farming where crops are grown devoid of soil. Analogous to soil, the primary function of these substrates is to provide plant anchoring and growth along with adequate nutrient flow, suitable temperature, pH, and microbial community (Rajan et al., 2019; Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). Furthermore, it supplies water and oxygen required for the healthy growth of the plants. Soils have strong cation exchange capability that balances the nutrients available for plant growth. This is absent in hydroponics where plant roots are directly exposed to a nutrient solution. A growth substrate with cation exchange ability can overcome this shortcoming of vertical farming (Van Gerrewey et al., 2021). Such a substrate is essential, particularly for plants with deep root system, those with tall heights, and vining plants (Xi et al., 2022). A material should possess the following characteristic to be used as a growth substrate for vertical farming: The substrate should have an inherent porous structure with a balanced distribution of large and small pores to supply adequate water and oxygen and should be non-toxic in nature (Rajan et al., 2019; Savvas, 2003). Substrates with high water retention capacity reduces water losses and provides effective water management. The conventionally used substrates in urban farming practices are rockwool, perlite, pumice, expanded clay, polyurethane foam, coir dust, etc. A schematic diagram (Fig. 9 a) illustrating plant growth supported on a porous substrate is shown in Fig. 9 along with some of the potential growth substrates employed in vertical farms.

Zeolites are naturally occurring, alkaline-hydrated aluminosilicates having a porous structure (Mondal et al., 2021) as shown in Fig. 9(b). Use of zeolites as substrates (zeoponic substrates), started way back in 1990s. Their high porosity (Leggo, 2000) combined with the capability to exchange cations present with the competing ions from interacting solutions (ion-exchange) make them attractive candidates as slow-release fertilizers (Xi et al., 2022; Eroglu et al., 2017; Natural Microporous Materials in Environmental Technology 1999; Ramesh & Reddy, 2011). Zeoponic substrates mainly use clinoptilolite for plant culture due to its selectivity for K + and NH4 +. In 1990, National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) first attempted to grow plants in space by mixing clinoptilolite with phosphate rock mixtures as well as with synthetic apatite with an objective of providing all growth nutrients in one go (Natural Microporous Materials in Environmental Technology 1999). Recently, Nanyang Technological University (NTU) and Panasonic Factory Solutions Asia Pacific, Singapore attempted to adapt this concept of providing entire nutrients in a single go by decorating zeolites with metal nanoparticles and easily protonated polymers. They observed an improved plant growth due to the adsorption of anions combined with the cation exchange (Xi et al., 2022). Completely organic zeolites were produced and employed in crop production by Leggo et al. on composting a mixture of crushed clinoptilolitic tuff with poultry manure (Leggo, 2000). Several studies comparing various substrates cite the importance of zeolites in contributing towards improved plant growth, higher mineral content and reduced potassium leaching (Gül et al., 2005). However, in spite of having the potential to provide entire growth nutrients and being organic, the application of zeolite is limited to that of a soil modifier. Prior to its intensive use, detailed research to evaluate the risk of leaching toxic surfactants loosely attached to the zeolite surface is necessary (Nakhli et al., 2017).

Another growth substrate used is hydrogel obtained from superabsorbent polymers. Hydrogels are three-dimensional networks with low crosslink density formed by hydrophilic polymers that can swell in water (Ma et al., 2023; Guilherme et al., 2015). A possible molecular structure of a hydrogel is given in Fig. 9(c). They can hold a high fraction of water (usually higher than 90%) between their macromolecular chains while maintaining its structure because of the physical and/or chemical crosslinking of individual chains (Guilherme et al., 2015; Bahram et al., 2016). Superabsorbent hydrogels are polymers with high water absorbance and retention capability due to the hydrophilic groups present along its chains. This facilitates enhanced water and nutrient supply for the plants. Hydrogels can be prepared from both, natural (cellulose, starch, chitosan, alginate) and synthetic materials (e.g. polyacrylic acid and polyacrylamide) and can be crosslinked either chemically or physically. This provides the possibility for tuning its water absorption and degradation properties (Xi et al., 2022; Palanivelu et al., 2022). Ease of handling is yet another advantage due to its light-weight nature.

Luan et al. reported the use of oligoalginate immobilized hydrogel in a hydroponic system for lettuce and Chinese mustard cultivation by grafting cross-linked polyacrylamide (PAM) chains onto carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) using γ-irradiation (Luan & Xo, 2017). Further, biodegradable hydrogels derived from non-toxic cellulose-based materials, such as cellulose derivatives, sawdust cellulose, rice ash, wheat straw, pineapple peel etc. are receiving increased attention in recent past (Palanivelu et al., 2022). For instance, bio-based hydrogel derived from graft copolymerization of okara with acrylic acid and acrylamide increased the yield of leafy vegetable choy sum (Zhu et al., 2020), whereas the hydrogel from cotton gave better yield of Capsicum annuum (Hafiz-Afham et al., 2023). Such hydrogels are non-toxic and provide good sustainability. Nanomaterials can further add to the substrate certain characteristics owing to its unique physicochemical properties and higher absorption and penetration rates. Imalia et al. attempted to develop a substrate based on hydrogel present in diaper waste and by adding straw nanofibers to it for the cultivation of beans (Imalia et al., 2020). Such bio-based hydrogels can promote effective waste management besides being promising growing substrates for the implementation of sustainable vertical farms. However, bio-based hydrogels are less stable mechanically and are prone to easy degradation demanding continuous replacement. While the synthetic hydrogels such as acrylic acid and polyacrylamide will not degrade but their overuse may release biologically toxic residues to the environment (Palanivelu et al., 2022; Song et al., 2020).

Textile fabrics are another prospective material that can replace the conventional substrates used in vertical farms. Non-woven polypropylene, nylon and polyester meshes, electro-spun nanofibers mats from polyamide and polyacrylonitrile have been investigated for algal attachment and harvesting algae (Fricke et al., 2022) . Such fabrics predominantly used in algae farming can be adapted to indoor vertical agriculture as well. When used for plant cultivation, it provides with the advantage of tailoring the shape and chemistry of textile fabrics in accordance with the root of each plant (Bielefeld University of Applied Sciences 2019). Fig. 9(d) depicts growth of grass supported on a textile fabric. Availability of fibers with different structures and chemical compositions and the possibilities of creating 1-D, 2-D and even 3-D fabrics extends its applicability as successful growing substrates. Further, relatively inexpensive and long-established textile production technologies are an added advantage (Bielefeld University of Applied Sciences 2019). Salam et al. observed superior plant growth on woven fabric as compared to the non-woven and knit type due to the trapping of plant roots within the inter-stitches of warp and weft yarns (Salam et al.,). The hairy textile surface provided by weft-knitted plush were also found to be efficient in supporting plant growth (Diestelhorst et al., 2021). To conclude, we foresee an increased acceptance of the soilless farming concept in households for local consumption by introduction of such low cost and readily available textile fabrics. One of the main drawbacks of these textile substrates are related to their disposal. Recycling and reuse of textile fabrics are highly recommended facilitating waste reduction and economics. Considering the quality of the recycled product and the energy and time expended for the recycling process, sustainable and bio-degradable textile fabrics are replacing the conventional textile grow substrates. The application of such innovative substrates in vertical farms is further discussed in Section 6.3.5. We appreciate the use of bio-based, degradable substrates considering the quality of products and sustainability of vertical farms.

The potential of the flourishing technology, additive manufacturing and 3D printing in mimicking nature can be explored to create substrates that replicate soil. Existing studies focus on printing structures with different pore geometries using Nylon 12 (Otten et al., 2012) and acrylic resins (Dal Ferro & Morari, 2015). However, the above mentioned polymers are non-degradable and therefore not preferred for growing crops. Printing hydrogels can be an ecofriendly alternative for biodegradable substrates and can be achieved by either seeding post-printing or developing a bio-ink with plant cells loaded into the polymer. For example, a 3D printed alginate-based scaffold embedding microalgae is shown in Fig. 10 . Use of polymer composites as growing substrates were also explored as a part of the f4f project. Fricke et al. investigated the effect of various polymer composites in cultivating macroalgae and rearing crickets and found bio-polymer composites based on polylactic acid and cellulose to be the best potential candidates as depicted in Fig. 11 (Fricke et al., 2022). Thus, bio-based substrates provide an upper hand for cultivation of both crops and insects.

Film farming is another innovation by Mori employing ‘hydromembrane’, a combination of hydrogel and membrane technology commercialized under the brand name Imec (Mori, 2013). The ‘hydromembrane’ is a thin hydrogel film which absorbs water and nutrients but filters out pathogens thereby protecting the plants from diseases. The water and nutrients are absorbed by the plants through an osmolality gap achieved by producing higher concentrations of sugar and amino acids (Mori, 2013; Z. Zhang et al., 2020). For film farming to be viable, it was proposed to be integrated with vertical farming through a cost-benefit analysis of a theoretical VF by Palanivelu et al. (2022); Zhang et al. (2020).

The film farming technology mentioned before was used for cultivating tomato, cucumber, melon, paprika, and lettuce (Mori, 2013). The farmland contaminated with sludge of tsunami in the Great East Japan Earthquake was recovered using Imec technology and an Imec tomato farm (9,000 m²) were established with the support of the Government in Chiba, Japan (Mori, 2013). Growing tomatoes using Imec technology was also tested in the desert of Dubai. This resulted in an improved productivity and quality due to the strong sunlight, long daytime and weather conditions (Mori, 2013). Some of the urban rooftop farms such as The Brooklyn Grang in New York City uses ‘Rooflite soil’, soil with organic matter compost, expanded shale, and spent substrate from mushroom production, commonly made of animal manure and lignocellulosic materials such as cereal straws and saw dust (Al-Kodmany, 2018; Harada et al., 2018). The aeroponic farm, AeroFarms in New Jersey has patented, a reusable cloth medium made from bisphenol A (BPA) free, post-consumer recycled plastic for seeding, germinating, growing, and harvesting (AEROFARM). Each substrate is made from 350 to 500 ml water bottles thereby eliminating them from the waste stream. The cloth can be reused after sanitisation without the risk of contamination, acting as a barrier between the mist and the plants (AEROFARM; Williamson, 2019). Recently, a biodegradable substrate fiber has been developed by Klasmann-Deilmann and Maan known as Nygaia (Nygaia). These are made from bio-based natural materials and hence degrade completely without any residue imparting sustainability (Nygaia).

Artificial lighting is important for plants grown in low light or no light conditions as in case of indoor vertical farms. Such a lighting is in fact used to replace the natural sunlight available to the plants grown outdoors. In vertical farming where crops are grown in closed chambers, artificial light is the primary source of energy stimulating photosynthesis and many other physiological processes related to plant growth and development (Ahmed et al., 2020; Bayat et al., 2018). Light is very important for life particularly for plants starting from their seed germination to flowering. The visible spectrum of white light between 400 and 700 nm is particularly crucial and is known as photosynthetically active radiation (PAR), represented by Fig. 12 (a). Light is also found to have influence on plant morphology, nutrient content, metabolites, flavor, growth rate, fruiting, flowering, color, and their overall health (Neo et al., 2022; Ruangrak & Khummueng, 2019; X. Zhang et al., 2020). Plants are found to adjust their growth and development in response to different light characteristics such as intensity, spectral composition, duration and direction (Bayat et al., 2018; Fiorucci & Fankhauser, 2017; Wong et al., 2020). Optimum balance between blue and red light is required for plant growth and development as plants use blue light to develop chlorophyll and red light to initiate photosynthesis (Avisomo). Hence, blue light primarily affects leaf growth while red light supports fruiting and flowering (Neo et al., 2022; Wong et al., 2020; Avisomo). The enhanced growth of lettuce under blue/red spectra as compared to those grown under white light spectra can be appreciated from Fig. 12. One of the challenges of vertical farming is in providing artificial lighting to substitute natural sunlight.

Gas-discharge lamps (GDLs) such as fluorescent lamps (FLs) and high-intensity discharge lamps (HIDLs) were conventionally used to grow plants in the absence of sunlight (Dutta & Ibaraki, 2006). As artificial light involves higher power and operational costs, modern technology introduces energy efficient LEDs to replace these conventional light sources. LEDs possess higher power efficiency, i.e., 45% higher than high-pressure sodium lamps. Their lower heat dissipation combined with smaller size facilitates placement of maximum tiers closer to the plants. Their longer life span, low operational costs and environmental friendliness makes them ideal candidates for artificial lighting. Application of LEDs have grown to such an extent that Verified Market Research predicts a rise in global LED grow light market from USD 1.50 billion in 2021 to USD 14.16 billion by 2030 (Verified Market Research 2023). These figures are certainly an indication of the current transition towards indoor agriculture.

LEDs are specialized diodes that convert electrical energy into light and thereby provides effective lighting to the plants. The LEDs emission wavelength of light is based on the particular semiconductor material (Nair & Dhoble, 2015) and hence provides for tuning its spectral composition and intensity. Spectral composition of light refers to different wavelengths present in the light source and it varies with the light source (Thosar). The provision to tune LED spectral composition and intensity is highly advantageous in agriculture, as plants require specific wavelengths for their growth and metabolism. For instance, red and blue light drives photosynthetic metabolism whereas far-red light controls the gene expression, plant architecture, and reproductive responses (Wong et al., 2020). In addition, photoperiod (the time duration in which plants receive light in a day) and light quality is also known to impact plant growth and responses (Wong et al., 2020). This is because plants require a period of light and darkness known as photoperiod to regulate their mechanism, calculated in a daily cycle of 24 h as the earth rotates (Nájera et al., 2023). Photoperiod deficiency in plants with high light requirements can produce growth imbalance along thereby affecting the productivity and quality of a VF system (Nájera et al., 2023; Craufurd & Wheeler, 2009). With the advent of nanotechnology, there arises a possibility to engineer LEDs to produce specific plant responses by tuning the composition of phosphors. Phosphor is a solid inorganic luminescent material that emits light on exposure to an external energy source (Phosphor). LEDs when coated with such phosphors can emit a specific color by absorbing light from the LEDs and the resultant colour depends on the composition of the phosphor. For instance, white LEDs made by coating blue LEDs with phosphor, emitting longer wavelength light such as those in the red region by absorbing a blue light photon (Intematix). Use of phosphor converted LEDs can improve the photosynthesis efficiency of plants by matching the emission of LEDs with the absorption of chlorophyll pigments. Several doping systems such as Eu2+, Ce3+, Cr3+, and Mn4+ have been explored for fabrication of such LEDs. There are several researches focusing on developing red-emitting Mn4+ activated phosphor-converted LEDs for indoor plant growth such as Sr3LiTaO6:xMn4+ (Yang et al., 2021), CaMg2La2W2O12:Mn4+ (Ma et al., 2021) LiLaMgTeO6:Mn4+ (Li et al., 2020), CaSrMgWO6:1%Mn4+ (Deng et al., 2022) etc. Huang et al. observed an improved fresh weight and reduced nitrate content in Lactuca sativa grown in an aquaponics system employing Eu2+-doped CaAlSiN3 LEDs for lighting (Huang et al., 2023). Hence, chemical modification of LEDs can improve the yield from a vertical farm by assisting plant growth.

We also propose the use of light harvesting materials to improve the photosynthetic efficiency of plants. Light harvesting materials absorb white light and emit visible light imperative for plant growth. Researches have successfully created artificial photosynthesis systems using TiO2, Au, Ag, Mn, and CeO2 nanoparticles, single-walled carbon nanotubes and CdTe semiconductor quantum dots (Xu et al., 2020). They can be integrated to any component of the vertical farm or plants such as outer cover, growing trays, plant leaves, stem, roots etc. For instance, spraying polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) encapsulated carbon dots (CDs) onto hydroponically grown lettuce leaves improved the rate of photosynthesis and production yield as reported by Xu et al. (Xu et al., 2020). Further, an increased biomass and chlorophyll content were observed in mung beans by using antioxidant red emissive CDs/PVA film (doped with ascorbic acid) for greenhouses as shown in Fig. 13 (Li et al., 2021). We presume that incorporation of such harvesting materials into outer cover or shelf made from transparent composite as proposed in Section 6.1 and 6.2 can effectively conquer natural sunlight as well as LED light increasing plant growth and yield. Hence, application of nanotechnology not only facilitates production of growth substrates but also ensures effective utilization of light.

As already discussed, artificial lighting comes with the challenge of higher energy requirements involving higher cost and resources. Therefore, various strategies ensuring maximum light uptake with limited wastage is necessary. Interplant lighting, upward lighting, and reflectors are used successfully to ensure effective light uptake by plants and to minimize the effects of shading (Avgoustaki & Xydis, 2021; Dueck et al., 2006). Considering the sustainability and economics of vertical farm, intermittent lighting strategy using pulsed LEDs is also employed (Olvera-Gonzalez et al., 2021). Another important advancement is in developing hybrid solar lighting systems using photovoltaics to compensate the high costs and reduced efficiency associated with artificial lights. Considering the efficiency of natural light in stimulating plant growth, harnessing sunlight was explored in combination with artificial light. Daylighting is used widely, which involves delivering natural light inside a building through an opening. Further, there have been attempts to generate electrical energy from sunlight using photovoltaic arrays (Fraas et al., 2002,144) and to distribute sunlight using hybrid luminaires system (Earl et al., 2003). Heliostats, solar tubes, and skylights initially used for light delivery are currently replaced by optical fibers giving rise to optical fiber daylighting system (OFD). In an OFD, solar collectors are coupled to an optical fiber made of polymer or silica that concentrates light and transmits into the building (Asiabanpour et al., 2018). As discussed previously, fluorescent materials are known to improve the photosynthetic efficiency and plant growth. An increase in yield of about 35% were predicted by Yalçın and Ertür on employing fluorescent reflectors produced by doping optical glass with fluorescent pigments from K2SiF6:Mn4+ in a solar illuminated vertical farm (Yalçın & Ertürk, 2020). The sunlight transmitted through the optic fiber bundle were directed to this reflector that converts blue-green light to red thereby improving the photosynthesis rate of plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applications and environmental considerations of lighting technologies
ce:section.ce:para: Energy-efficient and tunable LEDs can improve the yield and quality of the crop cultivated in a vertical farm. Tuning of the plant phenotypes were also found to be possible by controlling the spectral output of LEDs (Carvalho & Folta, 2014). Following this, Carvalho and Folta noted that lettuce with red, green, or green with red speckled leaves were obtained, depending on the light spectrum used (Carvalho & Folta, 2014). A 6-channel LED module system with precise software control were introduced by the German lighting solutions company Osram (Neo et al., 2022, Osram). This allows the growers to finely tune the light recipe of a particular crop by selecting a particular wavelength and intensity. In addition, lettuce produced using controlled light spectrum in an indoor farm was found to have a more sweeter and flavourful taste than that grown under natural sunlight in a greenhouse (Neo et al., 2022; Meng et al., 2020). Therefore, tunable spectrum LEDs can customize the light recipe imparting faster growth and improved yield along with manipulation of the chemical profile and appearance of the leaves (Gupta, 2017).

Considering the implication of color conversion quantum dot on the yield, NASA found that lettuce grown under orange and red quantum dot films gave better yield than those grown without these color conversion films (Parrish et al., 2021). Along the quality and yield obtained, energy required for lighting was given utmost consideration given its impact on the environment and cost. Green Spirit Farm in New Buffalo uses induction lighting technologies to simulate the spectrum of sunlight (Al-Kodmany, 2018; Matuszak). Here, light uses an electro-magnet to excite argon gas replacing filaments. This results in lower energy consumption with an improved life span of 100,000 h which is twice as that of an LED (Al-Kodmany, 2018). As these lights produce less heat, it can be placed closer to the plants minimizing energy wastage. The heat produced from the lights are further used for heating the building providing temperature regulation resulting in resource management (Al-Kodmany, 2018). LEDs with higher efficiency of 68% were produced by Dutch lighting engineers at Philips thereby reducing the expenses associated with lighting technologies. Further, a new lighting technology invented by a Dutch-based group, PlantLab, uses LEDs solely emitting the optimum wavelengths for plant growth (M. Levenston). This provides conservation of energy for the wavelengths which are not used and thereby improves sustainability.

Some of the drawbacks when artificial lights are used for growing is non-uniform light distribution, higher energy inputs and associated lighting costs. To address the problem of shading, Phillips introduced a product called GreenPower LED interlighting module to provide side lighting (Philips GreenPower LED Interlighting System). MechaTronix developed a product called CoolGrow emitting diffuse light of lower power ensuring uniform light (CoolGrow®). Also, in such cases, light can be placed closer to the crops. It is estimated that a power of 286 kJ = 79 Wh is required to generate one mol PAR light using the best LEDs available in the market. Accordingly, an electricity consumption of 332 kWh m − 2 y − 1 is necessary to obtain a production of 91 kg m − 2 y − 1 exposed to light intensity of 200 μmol m − 2 s − 1 for a photoperiod of 16 h with a plant density of 25 plants per m2 (Stanghellini & Katzin, 2023).
