A combination of light reflector and diffuser panels were proposed by Choubchilangroudi et al. to deliver natural sunlight to the floor depth of a vertical farm preventing shadowing, reducing electricity consumption and promoting sustainability (Choubchilangroudi & Zarei, 2022). As discussed in the previous section, application of solid state lighting can also reduce energy consumption of LED lightings thereby improve energy efficiency. Solid state lighting technique facilitates pulsed light output with the possibility of tuning pulse width characteristics such as frequency and duty cycle with a fast switching speed.

Overall, the advancement in lighting technologies discussed in this section helps to reduce energy wastage, improve the yield and quality of the products, insect control and disease management. Further, it helps to remove the effects of shading and non-uniform light distribution. It provides lighting day and night and all the year round removing the inconsistencies associated with natural sunlight. The intensive energy requirements can be controlled to a greater extend by using solar harvesting materials, hybrid lighting system and pulsed LEDs. Hence, a tuneable lighting technology with the provision for spectral control and minimum energy wastage, uniform light distribution blended with solar light capture can be promising for the vertical farms.

Vertical farming being a combination of controlled environment agriculture and soilless cultivation, monitor and control become the key factors for its successful operation. A controlled environment is very important to ensure maximum yield with minimum use of resources and to produce high quality crops. Each plant species requires different growing conditions that needs to be regulated to ensure optimum growth and development. Further, regulation of the indoor climatic conditions enables year-round production of seasonal crops. The environmental conditions, such as air temperature, humidity, gas concentration such as CO2, nutrient level, water temperature and quality, pH and light intensity needs to be controlled externally in a vertical farm for achieving optimum plant growth (Srivani et al., 2019). Moisture conditions and temperature need to be controlled for successful plant growth, as different plants require specific temperatures for photosynthesis (Kabir et al., 2023). It is also estimated that a vertical farm requires at least 8 to 10 h of artificial light per day to support healthy plant growth, and this further varies with the plant species (Kabir et al., 2023). Plants also require nutrients in different amounts at various growth stages for growth and functional maturation (Trejo-Téllez & Gómez-Merino, 2012). Hence, nutrient levels need to be controlled and optimized precisely as excess of stock solution in nutrient mixing tank makes target solution toxic, while inadequate flow can cause nutrient deficiencies (Kabir et al., 2023). Various systems such as passive and active ventilation, shadings, evaporative cooling systems, refrigeration, dehumidification etc. are commonly used to control environment in vertical farm (Vatistas et al., 2022). Combined with parametric control, monitoring them becomes substantial considering the energy input and economics of the raw materials employed. Conventional farming involves manual monitoring and control, which requires human labor and destructive methods. However, manual monitoring and control of vertical farms can result in reduced efficiency and increased labor cost with higher chances of crop failure. The need for precise monitoring and control is a challenge for the expansion of vertical farming technology.

Recently, agriculture has become ‘smart’ with the introduction of Internet of Things (IoT) utilizing actuator and sensor technology to control plant growth parameters (Mehra et al., 2018; Siregar et al., 2022). IoT refers to an interconnected network of physical devices, vehicles, appliances and other physical objects, embedded with sensors, software and network connectivity facilitating collection and sharing of data (IBM). IoT includes sensors and actuators where sensors are devices that detect changes in the environment and produces an output signal whereas actuators are devices that converts the input signal or energy received into mechanical motion (Grant Maloy Smith; An Expert Guide to Actuators in IoT 2023). For instance, temperature sensors in an IoT collects the temperature data while actuator triggers heating or cooling function depending on the required temperature (An Expert Guide to Actuators in IoT 2023). Hence, IoT facilitates automated monitoring and control of vertical farms. The current state of the art in monitoring, optimization and control is artificial intelligence (AI), the booming technology of the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IoTs and AI for environment control and monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: IoT technology is transforming with the integration of AI. AI can effectively reduce human interventions by automatizing the processes in vertical farming through computer assisted monitoring, data processing and reporting. AI, together with robotics, IoTs and machine/deep learning have taken the idea of urban agriculture way ahead (Siregar et al., 2022; Saad et al., 2021). It also helps to predict and mitigate any kind of crop failure. IoT uses sensors and similar devices to sense changes in environmental conditions of farms such as light, temperature, pH etc. and simultaneously convert it into data. The data is then delivered to the controller using technologies such as ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Wi-Fi, near field communication, Bluetooth low energy, mobile-phone technologies, Ethernet, etc. (Gupta & Ganapuram, 2019). The controller processes the data and provides decision making analogous to human brains. Controllers are intelligent systems comprising cloud computing facilities, personal digital assistants, regulation equipment, and operating terminals. On analyzing the observations and reference data, simulation modeling is employed to predict optimum parameters required for better plant growth. The output commands are received by the actuators which then starts the action (Siregar et al., 2022; Saad et al., 2021; Gupta & Ganapuram, 2019). The role of technology in sensing, analyzing, predicting, and controlling growth parameters of a vertical farm can be appreciated from Fig. 1. An IoT system based on deep neural network using Raspberry Pi3, Arduino and Tensor flow were proposed to grow tomato plants by Mehra et al. (Mehra et al., 2018). Palande et al. developed a fully automated system known as ‘Titan Smartponics’ that serves as a cost-effective IoT network in hydroponics using various sensors, Arduino and an open-source automation software Domoticz and Raspberry Pi as a control unit (see Fig. 14 ). The software also provided integration of the cloud data over a mobile phone application (Palande et al., 2018). Image analysis and AI techniques were used by Pillutla et al. to monitor the growth of basil leaves through an algorithm leveraging the object segmentation model (Pillutla). Akateva et al. combined additive manufacturing along with microcontrollers and high-level programming language microPython to create an automated vertical farm (Akateva et al.,). A smart lighting system based on a Fuzzy logic controller was developed by Bernardo et al. for hydroponic cultivation of lettuce utilizing both LED and solar power energy (Bernardo et al., 2023). Further, a digital twin approach was used by Ghandar et al. using Dynamic Data Driven Application System (DDDAS) paradigm for a single production unit as well as for a set of farms (Ghandar et al., 2021). Although, IoT based sensors provide remote control of the growth parameters, a digital twin facilitates a virtual environment for integrating and managing these data. This allows coordination between vertical farms of the same city and to manage production in accordance with the specific demands.

A smart irrigation system known as ThinkSpeak IoT platform based on real-time analysis was developed by Lakshmiprabha and Govindaraju to monitor and control parameters for a hydroponic system. This includes water flow rates, temperature, and humidity (Lakshmiprabha & Govindaraju, 2023). They observed an increase in crop yield with high efficiency as compared to the conventional irrigation system. A New York-based vertical farm, Farm.One, utilizes a data-driven approach based on IoT sensors to monitor plant health, environmental control, and system performance (Vertical Farming Technology: IoT). This technology allows round-the-year production of rare herbs, edible flowers, and micro greens for restaurants. Infarm, an advanced agricultural enterprise in Germany, grows over half a million plants each year within a compact area of just 40 m2s by integrating AI and machine learning to their vertical farms (Vertical Farming Technology: IoT). It includes sensors and the data produced is constantly evaluated by their AI system while machine learning algorithms create predictive models that constantly adjusts the farming conditions to maximize yield. This facilitates 95% lesser use of land and water resources as compared to conventional farming. According to reports, Infarm's innovative techniques have played a significant role in promoting sustainability by conserving more than 130 million liters of water, sparing over 180,000 m2 of land, and cutting ca. 1.6 million food miles (around 3 million kilometers) from their production processes (Infarm Heralds the next Generation of Cloud-Enabled Farming). However, in spite of its achievements, it has faced a major setup recently in the year 2023 (This Vertical Farming Company).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy and economics of smart vertical farms
ce:section.ce:para: Smart vertical farms integrating AI with IoTs facilitate automated control of the production. They assist in maintaining uniform and optimum conditions essential for plant growth and development. Automation minimizes the manual errors incurred while crop health monitoring allows early detection of diseases and controlling them (Popkova, 2022). Hence, wastage of resources is controlled to a greater extend. Monitoring the amount of water and providing the right amount can lead to conservation of water resources. Also, monitoring of the environments and lighting requirements promotes energy regulation. Hence, the precision data-driven decision-making contributes to energy management and resource efficiency coupled with higher crop yield and enhanced yield quality (Kabir et al., 2023). Furthermore, cutting-edge automation and AI technologies promise cost reduction owing to the conservation energy and resources as well as human labor costs (Kabir et al., 2023; Csordás & Füzesi, 2023). However, the initial investments for implementing an AI system can be expensive, especially for small-scale growers, considering the hardware, software, expenses and the technical skills required to operate the system (Kabir et al., 2023).

As understood from the previous sections, higher cost and energy requirements are two main factors abating the growth of vertical farms. The energy demands for electricity can be handled by switching to renewable natural energy sources like solar, wind (Jiang et al., 2023), geothermal etc. (Avgoustaki & Xydis, 2020; Xydis et al., 2020). Installation of solar panels and windmills near to the farms can cater the energy requirements promoting eco friendliness, sustainability, and efficiency. Recent technologies have made solar and wind energy highly accessible and affordable (Jiang et al., 2023). It can be captured, stored and used as when required (Ahmad & Alam, 2018). This makes them more reliable and less susceptible to market fluctuations than fossil fuels (Renewable Energy Technology Unleashes Vertical Farming's True Potential). Electricity generation using perovskite solar cells (Vatti et al., 2020), triboelectric nanogenerators (Bamatov & Bamatov, 2020; Rahman et al., 2021), thermo-electric generators and energy stored batteries are viable sources of alternate energy. Ohab et al. developed an off-grid vertical farm to grow tomatoes where controlled environment was provided by solar photovoltaic devices with Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) technique (Ohab et al., 2022). Recycling solar panel energy in vertical farms can be an innovative approach. Here, the solar power generated by panels are used more than once (Renewable Energy Technology Unleashes Vertical Farming's True Potential). For instance, the solar energy captured by solar panels are used to power LED lights and the light emitted by grow lights are absorbed by the panels thereby creating as sustainable loop without wasting the energy (Ahmad & Alam, 2018; Renewable Energy Technology Unleashes Vertical Farming's True Potential). Further, vertical farms can be fueled by its own bio-waste, i.e., from biofuels produced by anaerobic digestion of such wastes. A biogas system to capture methane from plant waste and simultaneously convert it to heat, steam and electricity can be promising (Avgoustaki & Xydis, 2020; Atelge et al., 2020). This is already employed in the vertical farm of Chicago, ‘The Plant’, and are under proposal in many developing farms (United States Environmental Protection Agency). Vertical farming being a form of closed-loop agriculture allow re-circulation of waste water such as urine wastewater from domestic sources for irrigation. This minimizes the problem with domestic waste disposal along with provision of nutrients for the plants (D'ostuni et al., 2023). Also, sustainable industrial batteries and hydrogen power cells can be some of the future prospects in making vertical farms sustainable (The Potential for Renewable Energy in Vertical Farming). All of these renewable energy sources can reduce the consumption of fossil fuels thereby reducing greenhouse gas emissions contributing to the sustainability aspect of vertical farms (Renewable Energy Technology Unleashes Vertical Farming's True Potential). Harnessing renewable energy can minimize energy costs making vertical farms financially viable (Renewable Energy Technology Unleashes Vertical Farming's True Potential).

On quantifying the self-sufficiency potential of half a million homes in Adelaide, South Australia, Hume et al. discovered that almost 65% of residential properties possess enough land and resources to capture and store rainwater for irrigation, even in the dry year (Hume et al., 2022). Apart from catering the water needs for irrigation, water harvesting systems can provide energy for pumping raw water, relieve drainage, and reduce the risk of local flooding (Jurga et al., 2020). An average of 35.9% of the water needs required for hydroponic cultivation of lettuce in Wrocław, Poland was covered by a rainwater harvesting system thereby saving 146,510 L of water annually (Jurga et al., 2020). Furthermore, use of water-harvesting materials capable of collecting water from atmospheric humidity can cater water required for both irrigation and environment conditioning. These are particularly significant for vertical farms located in arid regions confronting water scarcity. Hygroscopic Cu-complexes with water absorption capacity of 3.0 g − 1 and water release rate of 2.24 g − 1 h − 1 have been used by Yang et al. to harvest atmospheric water by integrating it into solar panels to cultivate Baby Kangkong (Ipomoea aquatic) (Xi et al., 2022; Yang et al.,). Another prospective material in this regard is a mixture of metal–organic framework (MOF) and N-isopropylacrylamide aerogel with a water collection rate of 6.04 g − 1 (95% efficiency) (Xi et al., 2022; Yilmaz et al., 2020).

Smart materials are materials that can respond or adapt to different stimuli like light, heat, electric field, pH etc. in a controllable and reversible way. Application of smart materials in vertical farms can make them smarter and more sustainable. For example, smart substrates such as hydrogels with the potential to diffuse nutrients in response to a specific stimulus such as concentration gradient (Zhang et al., 2020) or substrates with inherent stimuli-induced degradation triggered by topological changes or bond cleavage (Lim et al., 2014). Substrates possessing stimuli-induced degradability can reduce the carbon footprint. Release and optimization of desired nutrients levels using responsive substrates promotes resource efficiency along with automated response. Phase change or thermochromic materials that can adapt to temperature changes are another category of smart materials to be used in vertical farms. They can be integrated within the outer covers, walls, windows, or roofs of the farms to reduce thermal fluctuations and control the temperature. Simultaneously, it can reduce energy loads required for heating and air conditioning thereby regulating the inside temperature. For instance, materials reflecting infrared light on hotter days and transmitting them on cooler days maintains temperature and can be quite promising (Xi et al., 2022). A significant innovation in this regard can be thermotropic windows as shown in Fig. 15 , developed by the department of Chromogenic Polymers, research division Functional Polymer Systems, Fraunhofer IAP, Germany. These windows exhibit optical transmittance switching between the hot and cool states with windows transmitting light completely at room temperature. This is because the thermotropic acrylate capsules dispersed within the acrylate-based resin matrix show matching refractive indices at lower temperature. Above a predefined switching temperature (usually 35 °C), the refractive index of the capsule changes and hence convert into scattering units making the windows opaque. Application of such windows in vertical farms can conserve energy. An extended innovation can be thermotropic outer covers. As the acrylate capsules provide the possibility of tuning refractive index, it can be matched with that of the already existing transparent polymer matrices such as poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), polyvinyl butyral (PVB), or ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) to produce thermotropic outer covers (Muehling et al.,). In addition, we propose the possibility of dispersing the capsules to the epoxy-based composites developed in Section 6.1 and 6.2 to fabricate energy-efficient covering materials and growing shelves. Similarly, thermo-responsive smart windows were developed by Zhou et al. using water-dispersed poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAm) hydrogel particles exhibiting a phase transition at the lower critical solution temperature (LCST). They were found to possess excellent thermal storage capability along with solar light modulation (Zhou et al., 2020). NTU, Singapore developed a nanosilicone based coating which blocks 90% of the heat resulting in increased yield of a hydroponic farm in terms of leaf width and leaf weight (Xi et al., 2022; Khew, 2016). Such smart materials that can tune optical properties and transmittance in response to the environment are prospective materials of the future vertical farms allowing simultaneous control of both heat and light. These smart materials provide energy efficiency and cost reduction by minimizing the heating and cooling requirements. This further contributes to resource management and sustainability.

Modern technology facilitates use of AI for forecasting and crop management. As pest and disease control is another major challenge in vertical farms: Computer vision (Obu et al., 2021), sensors, and drones (Singh & Yogi, 2022) can be used to locate pests and diseases and provide treatment promoting plant health. Also, predictive analytics can forecast the risk and spread of pest and disease outbreaks. Visual sensor technology provides comprehensive monitoring of crop growth through image-based analysis (Kabir et al., 2023; Schima et al., 2016). Sensors capture visual data while high-resolution cameras continuously monitor the crops providing real-time information on the growth patterns and pest or disease development. These data can be used advantageously for early detection of issues and treatment (Kabir et al., 2023). Drones can fly over the crops of vertical farm and capture images and collect data. For instance, AeroFarms (AEROFARM) uses the expertise provided by Nokia Bell Labs (Nokia Bell Lab) in autonomous drone control, private wireless networks, advanced imaging, sensor data pipelines, and AI technology for mobile sensors. The plant health monitoring in iFarm (IFarm) is attempted using computer vision technology that involves the application of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).

Application of robotics can produce automation in crop planting and harvesting, nutrient supply, artificial shading, shelf-movement etc. in modern vertical farms (Van Delden et al., 2021). AI-driven farm tools are faster, tougher, and more accurate than human labor (Sharma et al., 2022). Lauguico et al. developed a framework for optimizing a robotic arm used for harvesting crops in a vertical farm (Lauguico et al., 2019). Robotic arms are important as the crops are grown at larger heights in a vertical farm. Also, human intervention can disturb the controlled environment in a vertical farm. Improved crop quality with faster production cycles at lower cost is another advantage of robotics-controlled farms. Robots based on binocular vision (Wu et al., 2021), multi-sensory end-effector (Liu et al., 2007) etc. applied in modern agriculture can be easily accommodated to vertical farms. Marchant et al. developed a robotic and vision system to be used in CityCrop Automated Indoor Farm to plant and harvest crops without human interference. They also developed algorithms to predict and control the seeding and harvesting processes (Marchant & Tosunoglu).

A high-tech vertical farming system, Techno Farm, set up by the company Spread in Japan applies automation and robotics for seeding, growing, and harvesting crops (Vertical Farming Technology: IoT; CONNECTiNG LEAVES TO LiVES). This helps to increase the productivity and costs associated with human labor. Automation further improves the hygiene of cultivation environment producing lettuce with high quality and sustainability standards (CONNECTiNG LEAVES TO LiVES). Plantagon in Sweden is an automated farm with a helical structure for growing food. It employs a robotic belt that moves different plant rows starting from sowing to harvesting stage. The amount of sunlight received can also be controlled by such automated movements (Al-Kodmany, 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion and scope for future research
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, vertical farming has become the new face of modern agriculture promising food security for the future. The significance of vertical farming is highlighted throughout the review with a focus on current technological innovations in the field. An overview of the techniques practiced is presented followed by a comprehensive discussion on the material aspects. A brief insight into the implications of vertical farming is provided with a discussion on some successful vertical farming projects. These discussions establish a platform for advocating the role of vertical farms in fostering urban food security and resource management. Further, the review tracks recent innovations in the outer structure, cultivation shelves, growing substrates, light, and environment control of a vertical farm. Besides, we have given emphasis to cost-management and sustainability aspects of the vertical farm with energy efficiency and management being major factors addressed. Even, our attempt to replace conventional steel cultivation racks using the newly identified GBOV-glass fiber composite gives importance to economics and sustainability as it guarantees a lightweight, low-cost, transparent, and corrosion-resistant material. Lightweight and transparent polymer composites can form the material of vertical farm construction accounting for its higher specific strength and growth light guidance. Recyclable or degradable fiber growth substrates can reduce environmental waste and promote sustainability. Integration of nano-engineered LEDs, quantum dots, optical fiber daylighting technologies, smart hybrid lighting systems etc. into vertical farms can reduce the energy requirements associated with artificial lighting. At the same time, energy costs are also minimized. Furthermore, it is understood that intelligent materials, smart IoT sensors, AI, robotics, and machine learning have contributed deeply to the burgeon of smart vertical farms. Finally, the future perspectives and outlook on vertical farming is discussed recommending adaptation of prospective technological innovations such as harnessing energy from renewable sources, waste to energy conversion, harvesting, and smart materials. Addressing the challenges that impedes the acceptance of vertical farming locally by common man through advanced technologies can be a major breakthrough.

Considering the impact of vertical farms on the society, intensive research for developing multifunctional designs of vertical farms should be advocated where engineers, architects, and technology providers are involved in the process. Conducting pilot programs generating real time data and analyzing them using advanced software. Further, developing energy models with the intention of minimizing energy consumption accounting sustainability factors and a techno-economic study for calculating construction and maintenance cost will assist in predictive-analysis (Al-Chalabi, 2015). Considering the current scenario where vertical farms used only to produce leafy greens, herbs, strawberries, tomatoes, and peppers, advanced innovations are necessary to enhance yield and profit from growing wide variety of crops. As the major factor that slackens the growth of vertical farms is their reduced acceptance among common individuals, lack of information on the scope and relevance is a major issue. In such situations, effective governmental policies and protocols along with awareness/training is a prerequisite. Intensive educational and training programs to generate interest, impart adequate knowledge, and technical skills is considered mandatory. Srinivasan and Yadav have conducted a detailed research and suggested that investments in research and development, promoting collaborations and knowledge sharing among and between urban farmers and technical source providers can boost the acceptance of vertical farms (Srinivasan & Yadav, 2023). More policies and funding for research and development will deliver more technological innovations as discussed throughout Section 6 and 7. Creating awareness through nurseries and visitor centers, community engagement, participation of immediate stakeholders like members of the local community, public institutions, small-business owners can play a major role in upscaling vertical farms (Ramin Shamshiri et al., 2018; Srinivasan & Yadav, 2023; Dias et al., 2019; Petrovics & Giezen, 2022). Identification of vertical agriculture as one of the city development strategies globally and governmental policies aiming increased market access to vertically farmed products along with economic profit can be worth considering.
To conclude, we hope this review motivates successful adaptation and implementation of vertical farms both locally and for large-scale projects pertaining to the sustainability and economics.

We acknowledge that the present review is not a systematic analysis of the existing literature on vertical farming and does not involve a bibliometric analysis. Instead, it involves an in-depth qualitative review focusing more on the recent innovations in the field. Accurate information on vertical farms currently under operation are not presented as many projects are prototypes or are under implementation. Further, in USA, more than 60% of farms belong to small, local farmers, providing a rough estimate of the total vertical farms available. Such information can be decoded using a systematic statistical bibliometric analysis, thereby providing scope for future research. Despite these limitations, our review gives insight into the innovations transforming vertical farms into highly productive, automated, economically viable, and sustainable food production technologies. We believe that this review will help to achieve the sustainable development goal (SDG) zero hunger through another SDG goal sustainable cities and communities by incorporating the latest technical knowledge and expertise of the scientific community.

Swathi Erekath: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Holger Seidlitz: Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Validation. Monika Schreiner: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources. Christian Dreyer: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

The authors also thank Dr. Mathias Koehler, Dirk Dietrich and Tiberiu Tatai of Fraunhofer IAP for their assistance during the experimental work.
